Blind Front-to-Front Bidirectional A*: An Efficiency-Oriented Framework for Pathfinding in Partially Observable Grid Environments

15 min read
Just now

--

A comparative study of standard A and an optimized bidirectional variant under fog-of-war conditions*
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 1: A* and Bidirectional A* Maze Trial
Abstract
A* remains one of the most widely deployed pathfinding algorithms in robotics, game development, and network routing, largely because it guarantees an optimal path while narrowing the search space with an admissible heuristic. Its principal weaknesses, however, are well documented: heuristic dependency, high memory consumption from large open/closed sets, and search times that scale poorly as environments grow. This paper presents and evaluates a bidirectional variant of A* — implemented and tested from scratch in JavaScript using the p5.js framework — that expands simultaneously from the start and goal nodes, revealing terrain only as it is explored (a “fog-of-war” or blind-search model, rather than assuming full prior knowledge of the map). The implementation further incorporates a front-to-front heuristic, in which each frontier is guided toward the nearest node in the opposing frontier rather than toward a fixed endpoint, alongside a proximity-to-optimum termination condition and a typed-array (Float32Array)-based cost bookkeeping system to reduce memory overhead. Across 30×30, 45×45, and 60×60 grid trials with variable-cost terrain generated by layered Perlin noise, the bidirectional variant matched the path cost of standard A* in the large majority of trials while reducing runtime by roughly 40–60%, with a small number of outlier trials in which early termination produced a marginally suboptimal path. This paper details the algorithm's design, the iterative debugging process that shaped it, the experimental methodology, and the resulting performance data, and situates the approach within existing literature on bidirectional and front-to-front search.

1. Introduction
Robotic path planning is the problem of determining a collision-free route for an agent moving from a start location to a goal location, either with full prior knowledge of the environment (global/offline planning) or with the environment revealed incrementally as the agent explores (local/online planning) (MIT Fab Lab, n.d.). Applications span household robots, autonomous vehicles, disaster-response robotics, and warehouse logistics (Liu et al., 2023).
Among the algorithms used to solve this problem, A* (Hart, Nilsson, & Raphael, 1968) remains a standard choice because it combines the guaranteed-shortest-path property of Dijkstra’s algorithm with the directional efficiency of a heuristic function, typically Euclidean or Manhattan distance to the goal (Karur, n.d.). A node’s priority is determined by
f(n) = g(n) + h(n)where g(n) is the known cost from the start to node n, and h(n) is an estimate of the remaining cost to the goal. Provided h(n) never overestimates the true remaining cost (admissibility), A* is guaranteed to return an optimal path (Umar, 2019).
Despite this guarantee, A* has three recognized limitations: its performance is highly sensitive to the quality of the chosen heuristic; its open and closed sets can consume substantial memory on large maps; and, because it searches unidirectionally from a single origin, it must expand a search volume that grows roughly with the square of the path length (Umar, 2019; Dey, n.d.). This paper investigates one well-known mitigation — bidirectional search — and specifically a front-to-front variant, in which the heuristic drives each search frontier toward the other rather than toward a static target.
This project’s contribution is not a new algorithm in the theoretical sense — bidirectional and front-to-front search are established techniques (Dey, n.d.; Wikipedia, n.d.-a) — but a from-scratch implementation, debugging process, and empirical comparison built specifically to test whether a practical, lightweight version of front-to-front bidirectional A* could reliably outperform standard A* on variable-cost, partially observable terrain, without incurring the quadratic heuristic-computation overhead that the full front-to-front formulation is known to carry (Wikipedia, n.d.-a).

2. Background and Related Work
2.1 Map Representation
Two common representations of an environment for pathfinding are occupancy grids, in which each cell is marked passable or impassable, and configuration space, in which the physical extent of the robot itself is folded into the obstacle boundaries (Karur, n.d.). This project uses a discrete occupancy grid, with the addition of variable per-cell traversal costs (representing terrain such as rough ground or swamp) rather than a strictly binary passable/impassable model.
2.2 Dijkstra’s Algorithm and A*
Dijkstra’s algorithm (1959) finds the shortest path in a weighted graph by maintaining “visited” and “unvisited” sets and relaxing edge costs outward from the source until every node has been visited (MIT Fab Lab, n.d.). A* extends this by adding a heuristic estimate of remaining distance, which allows the algorithm to prioritize nodes that appear to be headed toward the goal and avoid exhaustively searching the entire graph (Karur, n.d.; Simplilearn, 2024). D* (1994) further extends A* with incremental replanning for dynamic environments (MIT Fab Lab, n.d.), though this project’s environment is static once generated, so incremental replanning was intentionally out of scope.
2.3 Bidirectional Search
Bidirectional search, sometimes called “two-end” search, runs two simultaneous searches — one forward from the start, one backward from the goal — and terminates when the two frontiers meet (Dey, n.d.). Because each of the two searches only needs to cover roughly half the distance, the combined search volume can be substantially smaller than a single unidirectional search over the same distance, particularly on large graphs (Dey, n.d.).
A naive bidirectional implementation, however, has a known weakness: because each frontier is still guided by a heuristic pointed at a fixed endpoint (the frontier’s own opposite terminus), the two searches can pass near one another without truly converging, and the algorithm may terminate at the first point of contact between frontiers rather than at the true optimal meeting point (Wikipedia, n.d.-a).
2.4 Front-to-Front Search
Front-to-front bidirectional search addresses this by replacing the static-endpoint heuristic with a cross-frontier heuristic, formalized as
f(u, v) = g0(u) + h(u, v) + g1(v)where g0(u) is the cost from the start to node u in the forward search, g1(v) is the cost from the goal to node v in the backward search, and h(u, v) estimates the cost of connecting the two frontier nodes directly, rather than estimating the distance to a fixed target (Wikipedia, n.d.-a). This draws each frontier toward the other, rather than toward a static point, which can prevent the premature or off-target convergence seen in naive bidirectional search.
The full front-to-front formulation, however, is computationally expensive: evaluating h(u, v) for every pair of frontier nodes scales roughly quadratically with frontier size, which can outweigh its benefits on all but the largest search spaces (Wikipedia, n.d.-a). This is the central tension that this project's implementation attempts to resolve.

3. Methodology
3.1 Environment Generation
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Figure 2: Perlin-Noise TerrainThe test environment is a discrete grid populated using two-layered octaves of Perlin noise (Perlin, 1983; Wikipedia, n.d.-b), which produces spatially coherent, natural-looking terrain rather than uniformly random noise. Noise values below a threshold are converted to impassable walls; intermediate ranges are mapped to two tiers of elevated traversal cost (representing, for example, rough or swampy terrain); and a small independent probability of additional obstacle placement is layered on top to increase structural complexity. Start and goal cells are always forced to be passable, positioned near opposite corners of the grid, and the terrain is reseeded on every run so that no two trials share an identical map.
3.2 Blind (Partially-Observable) Search Model
A deliberate design decision in this implementation is that neither search algorithm has prior knowledge of the map. In an initial version of this project, both algorithms operated on a fully revealed node grid — effectively a “god’s-eye view” of the terrain. This was changed to more accurately reflect real-world pathfinding, in which an agent typically does not know the cost or passability of a cell until it is within sensing range.
This was implemented by maintaining a separate “known” grid of nodes, each of which is flagged as unknown until the search algorithm expands into it, at which point the node's true wall status and cost are copied over from the underlying terrain map and the flag is cleared. Neighbor generation no longer filters out walls in advance; instead, walls are only recognized — and pruned from further expansion — once a cell has been discovered. This means both algorithms explore adaptively rather than exploiting map knowledge they would not realistically have.
3.3 Standard A* Implementation
The baseline algorithm is a conventional single-source A* search using Euclidean-distance heuristics, an open set searched linearly for the lowest-f node, and cost accumulation that accounts for variable terrain cost rather than a uniform step cost of 1. Path cost and search duration are measured using performance.now() and reconstructed via parent pointers once the goal is reached.
3.4 Bidirectional Implementation
The bidirectional algorithm (aStar2) expands two frontiers concurrently — one from the start node, one from the goal node — with independent cost bookkeeping for each direction (gStart/hStart/fStart and gGoal/hGoal/fGoalTo avoid the two searches overwriting one another's state, an issue identified early in development when both directions initially shared the same cost fields.
Each open set is maintained as an array kept in sorted order via binary insertion (insertSorted), so that the lowest-cost node is always at index 0 and can be retrieved in constant time — replacing an earlier version that scanned the entire array on every iteration.
At each iteration, one node is expanded from each frontier. A candidate meeting path is recorded whenever a node discovered by one frontier is found to have already been visited by the other. Rather than terminating at the very first such contact point — which early testing showed could produce a suboptimal path — the algorithm continues until the best candidate path found so far is less than or equal to the sum of the two frontiers’ current minimum. f-values, a termination condition adapted from standard bidirectional-search proofs of optimality (Wikipedia, n.d.-a). This directly addressed an early-termination bug in which the bidirectional algorithm occasionally returned a higher-cost path than standard A* despite both algorithms using an admissible heuristic.
Path reconstruction stitches together the forward path (start → meeting node) and the backward path (meeting node → goal) using separate parentStart and parentGoal pointer chains.
3.5 Front-to-Front Heuristic (Lightweight Variant)
Rather than implementing the full pairwise front-to-front heuristic — which was found, through direct complexity analysis, to scale roughly quadratically with the number of nodes in each frontier and was judged too expensive for a real-time context — this project implements a lighter approximation: each frontier’s heuristic estimates distance to the nearest currently open node in the opposing frontier, rather than to a fixed start or goal point:
function heuristic
